lines.target: A crowd had gathered outside Buckingham Palace, waiting for the Queen to appear on the balcony.
lines.base: Una muchedumbre se había reunido delante del Buckingham Palace, esperando que la Reina apareciera en el balcón.

lines.target: When she finally came out, the crowd shouted — God save the Queen!
lines.base: Cuando salió al fin, la muchedumbre clamó — ¡Dios salve a la Reina!

lines.target: Perplexed, a young German student took his grammar book from his pocket, opened it and said aloud:
lines.base: Perplejo, un joven estudiante alemán sacó su libro de gramática del bolsillo, lo abrió y dijo en alta voz:

lines.target: That's wrong! — You don't like the Queen?, came the ominous reply.
lines.base: ¡Eso es falso! — ¿No te agrada la Reina?, le preguntó alguien con tono amenazador.

lines.target: No, it's not that. Look, you say: the lifeguard saves the drowning man, right?
lines.base: No, no es eso. Mira, tú dices: el socorrista salva al nadador que se ahoga, ¿verdad?

lines.target: Yes, replied the crowd.
lines.base: Sí, respondió la multitud.

lines.target: Listen, son, the twenty thousand people here can't be wrong. Don't try to be too clever.
lines.base: Escucha, hijo, las veinte mil personas que se encuentran aquí no pueden estar equivocadas. No intentes pasarte de listo.

lines.target: If I were you, I wouldn't worry, added an old man.
lines.base: Si yo estuviera en tu lugar, no me preocuparía, añadió un anciano.

lines.target: Ah! There you go again!, cried the German. You say: when I was in London, but it should be "were you". Help! I'm going mad. Can someone help me?
lines.base: ¡Ah! Vuelta a lo mismo, crió el alemán. Tú dices: cuando yo estaba en Londres, pero debería ser "estuvieras tú". ¡Socorro! Me estoy volviendo loco. ¿Puede alguien ayudarme?

lines.target: If anyone here is a grammar teacher, insist that he tells me the answer.
lines.base: Si hay alguien aquí que sea profesor de gramática, insisto en que él me diga la respuesta.

lines.target: That should be "insist that he tell me", actually, said an elegantly dressed gentleman.
lines.base: Eso debería ser "insisto en que él me diga", en realidad, dijo un hombre elegantemente vestido.

lines.target: That's the last straw! Explain why. — Subjunctive, old fellow, came the reply.
lines.base: ¡Eso es el colmo! Explique por qué. — El subjuntivo, amigo, fue la respuesta.

lines.target: Other languages have it, though. It seems too affected, apparently. However, other languages use it.
lines.base: Otros idiomas lo tienen, sin embargo. Parece demasiado afectado, al parecer. Sin embargo, otros idiomas lo utilizan.

lines.target: You see, English does like things simple, and the subjunctive is a difficult subject matter.
lines.base: Usted ve, el inglés tiende verdaderamente a simplificar las cosas, y el subjuntivo es un tema difícil.

lines.target: First it's got the same form as the infinitive, without the to, of course.
lines.base: Primero tiene la misma forma que el infinitivo, sin el "to", por supuesto.

lines.target: That means that you only realize the subjunctive in the third person singular, you see.
lines.base: Eso significa que uno solo se da cuenta del subjuntivo en la tercera persona del singular, ¿ves?

lines.target: You also find it with verbs like to demand, to ask, or to request.
lines.base: También lo encuentra con verbos como exigir, preguntar o solicitar.

lines.target: When a person says: "I insist that...", "He demanded that..." —
lines.base: Cuando una persona dice: "Insisto en que...", "Él exigió que..." —

lines.target: we put a subjunctive after it: He insist that he tell me, or, The judge insisted that the witness answer the question.
lines.base: colocamos el subjuntivo después: Insisto en que él me diga, o, El juez insistió en que el testigo respondiera a la pregunta.

lines.target: Are you still with me? Before our perplexed student could answer, another voice interrupted:
lines.base: ¿Sigues entendiendo? Antes que nuestro perplejo estudiante pudiera responder, otra voz intervino:

lines.target: Don't listen to that old fuddy-duddy!
lines.base: No escuches a ese viejo fósil.

lines.target: A crowd of curious onlookers had gathered to listen to the impromptu lesson.
lines.base: Una muchedumbre de curiosos se había agolpado para escuchar la improvisada lección.

lines.target: What this bloke says is all well and good in theory, but no one talks like that nowadays.
lines.base: Lo que dice este tipo está muy bien en teoría, pero nadie habla así en la actualidad.

lines.target: We find ways around the subjunctive. Here, take the examples that he gave you.
lines.base: Nosotros encontramos formas de esquivar el subjuntivo. Aquí, toma los ejemplos que ese viejo fósil acaba de darle.

lines.target: "I insist that he tells me" is OK, and "The judge insisted that the witness should answer the question"
lines.base: "Yo insisto en que él me diga" (sin subjuntivo) es perfectamente aceptable, y "el juez insistió en que el testigo debería responder a la pregunta" (sin subjuntivo)

lines.target: sounds much less pompous than the other sentence.
lines.base: suena mucho menos pomposo que la otra frase.

lines.target: I object, you said the older man, I insist that...
lines.base: Yo protesto, especie de rufián, dijo el hombre de más edad, insisto en que...

gap_candidates.target: A crowd had gathered outside Buckingham Palace, waiting for the Queen to appear on the balcony.
When she finally came out, the crowd shouted — God save the Queen!
Perplexed, a young German student took his grammar book from his pocket, opened it and said aloud:
That's wrong! — You don't like the Queen?, came the ominous reply.
No, it's not that. Look, you say: the lifeguard saves the drowning man, right?
Yes, replied the crowd.
Listen, son, the twenty thousand people here can't be wrong. Don't try to be too clever.
Ah! There you go again!, cried the German. You say: when I was in London, but it should be "were you". Help! I'm going mad. Can someone help me?
If anyone here is a grammar teacher, insist that he tells me the answer.
That should be "insist that he tell me", actually, said an elegantly dressed gentleman.
That's the last straw! Explain why. — Subjunctive, old fellow, came the reply.
Other languages have it, though. It seems too affected, apparently. However, other languages use it.
You see, English does like things simple, and the subjunctive is a difficult subject matter.
First it's got the same form as the infinitive, without the to, of course.
That means that you only realize the subjunctive in the third person singular, you see.
You also find it with verbs like to demand, to ask, or to request.
When a person says: "I insist that...", "He demanded that..." —
we put a subjunctive after it: He insist that he tell me, or, The judge insisted that the witness answer the question.
Are you still with me? Before our perplexed student could answer, another voice interrupted:
Don't listen to that old fuddy-duddy!
A crowd of curious onlookers had gathered to listen to the impromptu lesson.
What this bloke says is all well and good in theory, but no one talks like that nowadays.
We find ways around the subjunctive. Here, take the examples that he gave you.
"I insist that he tells me" is OK, and "The judge insisted that the witness should answer the question"
sounds much less pompous than the other sentence.
I object, you said the older man, I insist that...
